unitTitle: KS2 Y6 Variables in games (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Introducing variables
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a variable is something that can change
I can find examples of things that change (like a score)
I can change a score in a Scratch project
I can say that a variable can hold a number or letters
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: variable · change · name · value. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Score change Scratch project (ncce.io/scorechange) ready. Read the four 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–10) Starter — the football score. In a match the score and the time CHANGE; the pitch size stays the same. Pupils MULTI-SELECT what changes. A thing that can change is a variable.
2. (10–18) I-do → we-do — a variable can change. The score is a variable; it goes up and down. A variable holds ONE value at a time (one score at a time). Likely error: 'the pitch changes' → fix-words 'a variable is something that CHANGES.' Support choose what a variable is.
3. (18–32) Changing the score in Scratch. Show the change score by block: +2 makes it go up, -2 makes it go down. Demonstrate clicking the balls in Score change. Support choose what each block does; Challenge: what does -2 do?
4. (32–48) You-do — make a variable. Show the Scratch editor. Click Variables → Make a Variable → name it Score. Pupils ORDER the make-a-variable steps, SORT values into number/letters, then change the score and screenshot it.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what a variable is (something that can change).
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Variables in programming
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a variable holds one value at a time
I can name the two parts of a variable: its name and its value
I can choose a clear name for a variable
I can make and change a variable in Scratch
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: variable · name · value · set · change. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Scoreboard Scratch project (ncce.io/scoreboard) ready. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: a variable is something that can change.
1. (0–8) Starter — a game with variables. Show the score+timer game. The score and timer change; they are variables. Pupils choose the variables (the moving ball uses Motion, not a Variable block). Support choose how many values a variable holds.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do — one value at a time; name and value. A variable holds ONE value; a new value replaces the old. Every variable has a NAME (Score) and a VALUE (5). Likely error: 'it keeps both numbers' → fix-words 'the new value REPLACES the old one.' Pupils SORT names and values.
3. (22–34) A good name. Use the labelled-drawers idea: a good label finds things fast. Names should be short, say what they hold, no spaces (use _). Core: rename 'Number' to something better.
4. (34–50) You-do — make a scoreboard. Show the Scoreboard project (USA/Japan flags). Click Variables → Make a Variable; use change … by 1. Pupils ORDER the blocks (PARSONS), LABEL the score and timer on the stage, build the scoreboard and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil names the two parts of a variable.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt with the Variables button; do not make it for them.

lessons.title: Improving a game
lessons.objectives:
I can choose variables that would improve a game
I can set a variable to a start value with the green flag
I can decide where in the code to change a variable
I can improve a game using variables
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: variable · set · change · design · event. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Pong Scratch project (ncce.io/pong) ready. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: a variable is a name and a value that can change.
1. (0–8) Starter — improving Pong. Pong has a ball and a paddle but no score. You could add a score, a timer, or lives — all variables. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the variables to add (a 'new background colour' is not one). Support choose which is a variable.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — set the score at the start. Show the Pong code. set score to 0 with the green flag starts each game fresh. set PUTS a value in; change ADDS to it. Likely error: 'set and change are the same' → fix-words 'set PUTS a value in; change ADDS to it.' Pupils ORDER the green-flag blocks (PARSONS) and SORT set vs change.
3. (18–38) You-do — where you change the score matters. In setup → changes once; in the loop → races up; in 'if touching paddle' → only when the ball is caught. Pupils PREDICT on the activity worksheet (matching grid), then TEST each place in Scratch.
4. (38–50) Changing up and down. +1 up, -1 down; the score can change in more than one place. Core: up 1 then down 1 leaves it the same. Pupils add a working score and screenshot it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says where to put the block so the score goes up only when the ball is caught.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: predict first, then run — do not run it for them.

lessons.title: Becoming a games designer
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a games designer does
I can choose the artwork (sprites and background) for my game
I can write an algorithm for my game
I can explain my design choices
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: design · algorithm · code · sprite · background. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Fruit catcher remix (ncce.io/introfruit) and Fruit catcher star (ncce.io/fruitcatcher) ready. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: variables make a game more fun.
1. (0–10) Starter — a games designer. A designer PLANS a game before it is made: the sprites, the background, how it works. Show the controllers image. Try the Fruit catcher game; pupils MULTI-SELECT what they can see (bowl, falling apples, score — a racing car is the odd one out). Support choose what a designer does.
2. (10–22) I-do → we-do — choose your artwork. Choose TWO sprites to fall and a background. A sprite is a character/object; the background is the scene behind. Pupils SORT sprites vs backgrounds.
3. (22–42) You-do — write an algorithm. An algorithm is a precise set of steps. Show the star→bowl sketch. Pupils FILL-BLANK the falling-star algorithm and ORDER the algorithm steps. Likely error: 'an algorithm is the code' → fix-words 'an algorithm is the PLAN; code comes next.'
4. (42–50) Explain your design. Challenge: explain WHY you chose your sprites and background. Pupils screenshot their finished design (keep for next lesson).
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what an algorithm is.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt for ideas; do not design it for them.

lessons.title: Design to code
lessons.objectives:
I can add my artwork (sprites and background) to my game
I can choose a variable name that says what it holds
I can build my game from my design
I can test my game and fix problems (debug)
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: design · code · variable · name · test · debug. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Intro chatbot (ncce.io/introchatbot), the chatbot code (ncce.io/chatbotcode) and the Fruit catcher template (ncce.io/catchertemplate) ready; pupils' Lesson 4 designs to hand. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: you planned your game last lesson.
1. (0–8) Starter — a chatbot's variables. The chatbot remembers your name and age in variables, but they are called variable_a and variable_b. Pupils MULTI-SELECT where variables are used. Support choose why variable_a is a poor name.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — good names help. Rename variable_b → name, variable_a → age; now anyone can read the code. Likely error: 'the name does not matter' → fix-words 'a good name SAYS what it holds.' Pupils MATCH the old names to better ones.
3. (18–28) Build a small part. Pupils ORDER a short sequence (ask → set → say) as PARSONS, then start building their own game from their design (artwork + variables with good names).
4. (28–50) You-do — build, test and debug. Build from the design, run with the green flag, TEST against the design, then DEBUG (find and fix). Pupils ORDER the build/test/debug cycle and screenshot their running game. Challenge: what would you check if the score did not go up?
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what debug means.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not build it for them.

lessons.title: Improving and sharing
lessons.objectives:
I can find ways to make my game better
I can add a 'lives' variable to my game
I can share my game with others
I can give helpful feedback on someone's game
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: improve · evaluate · share · lives. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open; the Banana overload Scratch project (ncce.io/bananas) ready; pupils' games from Lesson 5 open. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. End-of-unit lesson — recap the whole journey at the end.
1. (0–8) Starter — are all games the same? Show four fruit-catcher versions. They all start the same but end up different; borrow good ideas. Pupils MULTI-SELECT ways to improve their game ('delete the whole game' is the odd one out). Support choose one real improvement.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — add a lives variable. lives counts the tries left; change lives by -1 when a sprite is missed; set lives to 3 at the start. Likely error: 'change lives by 1 lowers it' → fix-words 'to go DOWN, change by -1.' Pupils ORDER the blocks (PARSONS), then add lives to their game.
3. (20–35) You-do — share your game. Save → Share → copy the URL → give it to a friend. Pupils ORDER the share steps and screenshot their shared game.
4. (35–48) Helpful feedback. Say what you liked; say how it could be even better; be kind. Pupils choose the most helpful feedback and RATE their own confidence with variables (scale).
5. (48–55) End of unit — recap: introducing variables → variables in programming → improving a game → designing → design to code → improving and sharing. Self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil says what a lives variable does.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: model 'It would be even better if…'; keep feedback positive.
